content:
The fix was documented. The documentation was on a decommissioned wiki.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Cheaper than the hour you’ll lose next time this happens: verify your runbook links when you’re not on fire.
Friday 11:08 PM. Redis memory 78%. Growth rate 2%/hour. Page threshold 90%. SEV2 at 11:14 when we crossed 82%.
On-call opened PagerDuty incident. Clicked runbook link in alert annotation:
https://wiki.internal.old-domain.com/runbooks/redis-memory
HTTP/1.1 404 Not FoundNot DNS failure. Not auth. 404. Wiki migrated to Confluence six months ago. Alert annotation never updated.
11:55 PM. Memory 94%. Still reading Slack scrollback for “who knows Redis here.”
Runbook existed. New URL. Nobody updated the pager.
Confluence vs wiki — same runbook, wrong door
Old link (in PagerDuty): wiki.internal.old-domain.com/runbooks/redis-memory
Actual runbook (Confluence): confluence.internal/display/OPS/Redis+Memory+Emergency
Content: eviction policy steps, MEMORY PURGE caveats, when to fail over vs scale up. Good doc. Wrong pointer.
# What on-call ran while hunting
curl -I https://wiki.internal.old-domain.com/runbooks/redis-memory
# HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found# What existed
curl -I "https://confluence.internal/display/OPS/Redis+Memory+Emergency"
# HTTP/1.1 200 OK41 minutes finding the doc. 6 minutes executing it once found.
The six-minute fix we had all along
Senior engineer pasted Confluence link in Slack at 11:49.
11:51 — increased maxmemory, tuned volatile-lru policy per runbook section 3.
11:54 — identified rogue session keys from deploy Thursday (TTL missing).
11:57 — memory 71%. Stable.
redis-cli INFO memory | grep used_memory_human
# used_memory_human:6.82G (was 7.44G at peak)redis-cli --scan --pattern 'sess:*' | wc -l
# 2840000 (runbook: flush sess:* if deploy correlation + no TTL)Runbook section 4: deploy correlation checklist. Would have been minute one with a working link.
→ 30 Production Incidents That Cost $10K+ — runbook rot appears in case files where the answer existed and the path didn’t. Migration without link audit is an incident waiting for 11 PM.
Link audit vs hope — I tested both
Hope: engineers remember Confluence migration.
Audit: weekly cron hits every runbook URL in PagerDuty + Grafana annotations.
#!/bin/bash
# runbook-link-check.sh — runs Monday 06:00 UTC
while IFS= read -r url; do
code=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" "$url")
if [ "$code" != "200" ]; then
slack_post "#ops-alerts" "RUNBOOK 404: $url ($code)"
fi
done < runbook-urls.txtFirst run: 23 broken links of 89 total. 26% rot rate after wiki migration.
PagerDuty annotation update: bulk script. 2 hours one-time. Cheaper than one 41-minute memory cliff.
Grafana annotations vs PagerDuty vs runbook header
Three places links hide:
→ PagerDuty service custom details → Grafana alert annotation runbook_url → README in service repo (nobody opens during incident)
Single source of truth: service repo RUNBOOK.md with canonical URL. CI syncs to PagerDuty + Grafana on merge.
Three approaches we compared after Friday:
→ Manual wiki migration — high drift risk, 404 outcome → Repo RUNBOOK.md + CI sync — low drift, would have worked → Slack pin "updated runbooks" — medium drift, missed on-call
What the runbook said we skipped
Section 1: confirm memory metric (not CPU, not connections).
Section 2: check INFO stats evicted_keys rate.
Section 3: policy change procedure with rollback.
Section 4: deploy correlation — session TTL.
Section 5: escalate if > 95% after purge — page DBA.
We did sections 1–4 in 6 minutes once linked. At 94% we were one percentage point from section 5.
→ Production Incident War Room — service-by-service diagnosis commands include Redis memory path. Copy-paste when your wiki is dead.
Friday night economics
41 minutes at 11 PM on-call rate: irrelevant to salaried engineer.
Customer impact: cache miss storm elevated API latency 340ms p99 for 38 minutes. Checkout conversion dip estimated 0.4% — ~$1,100 Friday night revenue.
Six minutes with working link.
Link audit script: one afternoon to write. $1,100 one night. Do the contractor math.
What we shipped Monday
→ RUNBOOK.md canonical URL per service, CI sync to PagerDuty → Weekly runbook-link-check.sh with Slack alert on non-200 → Migration checklist item: "update pager annotations" blocking → On-call shift start: open runbook links for top 5 services, 30 seconds
Friday on-call wasn’t incompetent. Friday on-call was handed a 404 during a memory cliff. Verify links when calm. Not at 94%.
Shift-start ritual — thirty seconds
Every on-call shift now starts with:
./scripts/verify-runbooks.sh --services payments,redis,search,auth,checkout
# OK: 5/5 runbooks return 200
# FAIL: redis-memory -> 404 (pages ops)Friday would have failed at 6 PM handoff, 5 hours before memory cliff. Fix the link before the alert, not during.
The decommissioned wiki redirect that lied
wiki.internal.old-domain.com returned 404, not 301 to Confluence. Someone assumed redirect would save them during migration. It didn't.
Migration checklist item: old URLs must 301 or be deleted from alerts same day. 404 is worse than broken bookmark — it’s a false sense of “we migrated.”
Slack archaeology — how we actually found the runbook
11:12 PM — searched #redis history for "memory runbook". 47 results, newest 8 months old, all pointing to dead wiki.
11:23 PM — searched Confluence for “redis memory”. 12 results. Correct doc was result 4 — titled “Cache Layer Memory Management” not “Redis Memory Emergency.”
Naming mismatch. Even with Confluence, keyword search failed until senior engineer remembered the doc title at 11:49.
Fix: runbook front matter includes aliases: [redis memory, memory cliff, redis OOM] for search. PagerDuty link uses canonical URL only.
The session TTL bug that section 4 would have caught
Runbook section 4: deploy correlation checklist. Thursday deploy removed TTL from sess:* keys in session service.
redis-cli TTL "sess:abc123"
# (integer) -1 # no expiry — runbook says this is incident pattern11:51 PM flush of sess:* per runbook was aggressive but correct for incident. 2.84 million keys removed. Users re-logged in. Acceptable vs 94% memory.
Without runbook: we debated flush for 20 minutes while memory climbed. Section 4 says: “if TTL=-1 on sess keys post-deploy, flush sess:* with comms warning.”
Comms template for session flush existed in runbook appendix. We used it at 11:52. One sentence to status page. Would have been 11:15 with working link.
Cost comparison — link audit vs one Friday night
→ Link audit script development: 4 hours one-time → Weekly cron maintenance: ~0 hours (automated) → Friday night revenue + engineer overtime: ~$1,400 → Customer trust: unpriced
Cheaper than the hour you’ll lose next time this happens. We run audit Mondays. 3 broken links caught last month before they became Friday stories.
PagerDuty annotation archaeology
After Friday we audited 89 services. 23 broken runbook links. Pattern:
→ 14 wiki migration 404s → 6 Confluence renames without redirect → 3 typo in URL (extra slash, wrong space encoding)
Bulk fix script updated PagerDuty via API. 2 hours. One engineer. 23 future Fridays avoided maybe.
# pd_update_runbook.py — simplified
for svc in services:
url = repo_runbook_url(svc.name)
if requests.head(url).status_code == 200:
pd.update_service(svc.id, runbook_url=url)Runbook link is code. Treat it like code review.
Redis memory after fix — what we watch now
redis-cli INFO memory | grep -E 'used_memory_human|maxmemory|evicted_keys'
# used_memory_human:4.12G
# maxmemory:8.00G
# evicted_keys:847291Alert at 75% now, not 90%. Eviction rate derivative — not just level.
Friday runbook section 5 said escalate at 95%. We hit 94% before link worked. 75% gives runway for humans and docs.
Handoff note we added — runbook verified
RUNBOOKS VERIFIED (shift start):
- redis-memory: 200 OK
- payments-failover: 200 OK
- search-reindex: 200 OK30 seconds at shift start. Same six-field handoff template as deploy tracking. Incoming on-call cannot ack without checkbox.
Friday incoming on-call checked deploys. Didn’t check runbooks. Both are promises about what exists.
The session TTL deploy — Thursday’s landmine
Session service deploy Thursday 4 PM removed default TTL on sess:* keys. Friday 11 PM memory climbed.
Runbook section 4 documents this exact failure mode from 2024 incident. Would have been minute-one diagnosis.
404 didn’t just hide steps. It hid precedent. We solved this before. Nobody remembered without the doc.
Monday morning — link fixed before the postmortem
6:02 AM Monday. pd_update_runbook.py ran. Redis memory URL updated. Weekly cron green.
Postmortem at 10 AM included live demo: click PagerDuty link, 200 OK, section 4 visible.
41 minutes Friday became zero future Fridays if cron keeps working. Infrastructure boring enough to forget is infrastructure working.

Runbook rot is a trivia question on Froquiz until it’s a 404 at 11 PM — drill the Redis memory path before the pager fires. → Froquiz
